solutions.solution: Both are funny because the meaning of the term 'free speech' has twisted in both of them in a funny way. But the ideas are contrasting. I. 'free speech' means 'free use of telephone'; in II it means 'door'.
I is not funny because it's a normal response. It is funny because the momentum of 'order' as if the person were a waiter instead of a guest.
I is not funny: it is a normal reply. It is funny. It twists the ideas of 'spring'. 'spring' from 'streams' to 'the season of spring'.
Only I is funny. The way the conductor tells him that the bus stops at bottoms, makes the passenger look like a fool. But II is not funny; there is no element of humour in it.

Both are funny with contrasting ideas. The child notices that she has 'missed every time'; he doesn't realise that he was supposed to ask. Similarly, in II, the child accuses the man of trying to injure the lady; but the basic idea is the same in both the punchlines, which is the innocent style; therefore, but accents the word; the innocent. In I, the child has misunderstood the word; in II, he is condemning it.

Both are funny with the same idea. The foreman simply wants to know whether a job has been assigned to the apprentice. In both the scenes, we find that the mechanic works only when the foreman is nearby. So the basic idea in both of them.
I is not funny; it is a straightforward answer. II is funny: it twists the ideas meaning that the wife and the ear are both headaches for Prasad.

Only I is funny. It twists the conversation and implies that marrying a wife is a silly act. II is not funny; it is only an unexpected answer which the husband may have given out of ignorance or out of bad temper but certainly not out of humour.

Both are funny. In I, we find the meaning that Tinku's sister received engagement rings from three persons; in II we find that she has lost the ring. Both are funny but the idea is different in each of them.

I is funny because here the man highlights the word 'permit' in the doctor's sentence and says that she does it without his 'permission'. If II, the man highlights the word 'her own way' and says that only he is 'enough petrol'. Thus, both are funny but the ideas are contrasting.
